document_metadata.title: Furness General Hospital's proposed changes to face council probe
document_metadata.first_words: # Trust faces council probe over ward shake-up plans - Published A hospital trust's decision to potentially close or repurpose several wards without a public consultation is to be investigated by a group of councillors. Furness General Hospital, in Barrow, Cumbria, faces proposed changes as part of a review of beds at the site. University Hospitals of Morecambe Bay NHS Foundation Trust (UHMBT) said no consultation was needed because there were no changes or reductions to services, although locations may change to improve patient care.

chunks.content: A hospital trust's decision to potentially close or repurpose several wards without a public consultation is to be investigated by a group of councillors.
chunks.content_original: A hospital trust's decision to potentially close or repurpose several wards without a public consultation is to be investigated by a group of councillors.

chunks.content: Furness General Hospital, in Barrow, Cumbria, faces proposed changes as part of a review of beds at the site.
chunks.content_original: Furness General Hospital, in Barrow, Cumbria, faces proposed changes as part of a review of beds at the site.

chunks.content: University Hospitals of Morecambe Bay NHS Foundation Trust (UHMBT) said no consultation was needed because there were no changes or reductions to services, although locations may change to improve patient care.
chunks.content_original: University Hospitals of Morecambe Bay NHS Foundation Trust (UHMBT) said no consultation was needed because there were no changes or reductions to services, although locations may change to improve patient care.

chunks.content: Furness Locality Board - which ensures “local voices are heard“ - said it would start an investigation early in December about why public talks did not take place.
chunks.content_original: Furness Locality Board - which ensures “local voices are heard“ - said it would start an investigation early in December about why public talks did not take place.

chunks.content: Westmorland and Furness councillors have also called an “extraordinary meeting“ of the adults and health scrutiny committee over “significant concerns“ about the planned shake-up.
chunks.content_original: Westmorland and Furness councillors have also called an “extraordinary meeting“ of the adults and health scrutiny committee over “significant concerns“ about the planned shake-up.

chunks.content: Liberal Democrat Dyan Jones, committee chair, said members found out about the plans shortly before information became public.
chunks.content_original: Liberal Democrat Dyan Jones, committee chair, said members found out about the plans shortly before information became public.

chunks.content: “In these circumstances we felt it was necessary to take the unusual step of calling an extraordinary meeting to ensure these plans could be properly scrutinised,“ she said.
chunks.content_original: “In these circumstances we felt it was necessary to take the unusual step of calling an extraordinary meeting to ensure these plans could be properly scrutinised,“ she said.

chunks.content: At Wednesday's meeting, NHS representatives will be able to provide more details and explain the reasoning for their approach to public engagement.
chunks.content_original: At Wednesday's meeting, NHS representatives will be able to provide more details and explain the reasoning for their approach to public engagement.

chunks.content: The committee will consider whether to make recommendations for action, the Local Democracy Reporting Service said.
chunks.content_original: The committee will consider whether to make recommendations for action, the Local Democracy Reporting Service said.

chunks.content: Labour's Tony Callister, chair of Furness Locality Board, said he welcomed the meeting ahead of its own investigation.
chunks.content_original: Labour's Tony Callister, chair of Furness Locality Board, said he welcomed the meeting ahead of its own investigation.

chunks.content: Aaron Cummins, trust chief executive, said: “This review looked at how our beds are configured across the three main hospital sites and showed that there are opportunities for us to improve how we use them.“
chunks.content_original: Aaron Cummins, trust chief executive, said: “This review looked at how our beds are configured across the three main hospital sites and showed that there are opportunities for us to improve how we use them.“

chunks.content: “These proposed changes will help us ensure our patients receive the right care, at the right time, in the right unit; and reduce the time they have to stay in hospital.“
chunks.content_original: “These proposed changes will help us ensure our patients receive the right care, at the right time, in the right unit; and reduce the time they have to stay in hospital.“

chunks.content: He said staff had been consulted, as per trust policy, to give them the opportunity “to shape future plans“.
chunks.content_original: He said staff had been consulted, as per trust policy, to give them the opportunity “to shape future plans“.

class="ssrcss-jfvlft-ContributorContainer e19uhciu16"><div class="ssrcss-a7lnn6-Container-ContributorDetails e19uhciu13"><div class="ssrcss-12jkbjf-Text-TextContributorName e19uhciu6">Dan Hunt</div><div class="ssrcss-157fow5-Text e19uhciu10">Local Democracy Reporting Service</div></div></div><div class="ssrcss-jfvlft-ContributorContainer e19uhciu16"><div class="ssrcss-a7lnn6-Container-ContributorDetails e19uhciu13"><div class="ssrcss-12jkbjf-Text-TextContributorName e19uhciu6">Pamela Bilalova</div><div class="ssrcss-157fow5-Text e19uhciu10">BBC News, North East and Cumbria</div></div></div></div><div class="ssrcss-1rno81a-Divider e19uhciu15"></div></div><div data-component="metadata-block" class="ssrcss-1n2i08q-ComponentWrapper ep2nwvo2"><div><ul role="list" class="ssrcss-1q1gjvm-MetadataStripContainer eh44mf03"><div class="ssrcss-13nu8ri-GroupChildrenForWrapping eh44mf02"><li role="listitem" class="ssrcss-1dqo2s-MetadataStripItem eh44mf01"><div class="visually-hidden ssrcss-1f39n02-VisuallyHidden e16en2lz0">Published</div><div class="ssrcss-m5j4pi-MetadataContent eh44mf00"><span class="ssrcss-1pvwv4b-MetadataSnippet e4wm5bw3"><span class="ssrcss-61mhsj-MetadataText e4wm5bw1"><time data-testid="timestamp" dateTime="2024-11-22T06:45:58.992Z">22 November 2024</time></span></span></div></li></div></ul></div></div><div data-component="text-block" class="ssrcss-suhx0k-RichTextComponentWrapper ep2nwvo0"><div class="ssrcss-1o5p6v2-RichTextContainer e5tfeyi1"><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><b class="ssrcss-1xjjfut-BoldText e5tfeyi3">A hospital trust's decision to potentially close or repurpose several wards without a public consultation is to be investigated by a group of councillors.</b></p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Furness General Hospital, in Barrow, Cumbria, faces proposed changes as part of a review of beds at the site.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">University Hospitals of Morecambe Bay NHS Foundation Trust (UHMBT) said no consultation was needed because there were no changes or reductions to services, although locations may change to improve patient care.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Furness Locality Board - which ensures "local voices are heard" - said it would start an investigation early in December about why public talks did not take place.</p></div></div><div data-component="text-block" class="ssrcss-suhx0k-RichTextComponentWrapper ep2nwvo0"><div class="ssrcss-1o5p6v2-RichTextContainer e5tfeyi1"><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Westmorland and Furness councillors have also called an "extraordinary meeting" of the adults and health scrutiny committee over "significant concerns" about the planned shake-up.</p></div></div><div data-component="subheadline-block" class="ssrcss-1o1fzgd-ComponentWrapper-HeadlineComponentWrapper egtrm1f0"><h2 id="Proper-scrutiny" tabindex="-1" class="ssrcss-pbttu9-Heading e10rt3ze0"><span role="text">'Proper scrutiny'</span></h2></div><div data-component="text-block" class="ssrcss-suhx0k-RichTextComponentWrapper ep2nwvo0"><div class="ssrcss-1o5p6v2-RichTextContainer e5tfeyi1"><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Liberal Democrat Dyan Jones, committee chair, said members found out about the plans shortly before information became public.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">"In these circumstances we felt it was necessary to take the unusual step of calling an extraordinary meeting to ensure these plans could be properly scrutinised," she said.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">At Wednesday's meeting, NHS representatives will be able to provide more details and explain the reasoning for their approach to public engagement.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">The committee will consider whether to make recommendations for action, the <a href="/lnp/ldrs" class="ssrcss-f6h2dj-InlineLink e1kn3p7n0">Local Democracy Reporting Service</a> said.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Labour's Tony Callister, chair of Furness Locality Board, said he welcomed the meeting ahead of its own investigation.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Aaron Cummins, trust chief executive, said: "This review looked at how our beds are configured across the three main hospital sites and showed that there are opportunities for us to improve how we use them. </p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">"These proposed changes will help us ensure our patients receive the right care, at the right time, in the right unit; and reduce the time they have to stay in hospital."</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">He said staff had been consulted, as per trust policy, to give them the opportunity "to shape future plans".</p></div></div><div data-component="text-block" class="ssrcss-suhx0k-RichTextComponentWrapper ep2nwvo0"><div class="ssrcss-1o5p6v2-RichTextContainer e5tfeyi1"><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><i class="ssrcss-xbdn93-ItalicText e5tfeyi2">Follow BBC Cumbria on </i><a # Trust faces council probe over ward shake-up plans

![A blue and white sign reading Furness General Hospital is on the grass in the foreground. One of the hospital facilities is at the back. It is a two-storey brown brick building. There is another sign near it with directions to different parts of the hospital. The sky is grey. ](https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/ace/standard/562/cpsprodpb/7467/live/3cb6f970-a827-11ef-9c9f-85cfc89e7be6.jpg)Image source, LDRSImage caption, 

A council scrutiny committee will also meet to discuss the review of services at Furness General Hospital

Dan HuntLocal Democracy Reporting ServicePamela BilalovaBBC News, North East and Cumbria

- Published22 November 2024

**A hospital trust's decision to potentially close or repurpose several wards without a public consultation is to be investigated by a group of councillors.**

Furness General Hospital, in Barrow, Cumbria, faces proposed changes as part of a review of beds at the site.

University Hospitals of Morecambe Bay NHS Foundation Trust (UHMBT) said no consultation was needed because there were no changes or reductions to services, although locations may change to improve patient care.

Furness Locality Board - which ensures "local voices are heard" - said it would start an investigation early in December about why public talks did not take place.

Westmorland and Furness councillors have also called an "extraordinary meeting" of the adults and health scrutiny committee over "significant concerns" about the planned shake-up.

## 'Proper scrutiny'

Liberal Democrat Dyan Jones, committee chair, said members found out about the plans shortly before information became public.

"In these circumstances we felt it was necessary to take the unusual step of calling an extraordinary meeting to ensure these plans could be properly scrutinised," she said.

At Wednesday's meeting, NHS representatives will be able to provide more details and explain the reasoning for their approach to public engagement.

The committee will consider whether to make recommendations for action, the [Local Democracy Reporting Service](/lnp/ldrs) said.

Labour's Tony Callister, chair of Furness Locality Board, said he welcomed the meeting ahead of its own investigation.

Aaron Cummins, trust chief executive, said: "This review looked at how our beds are configured across the three main hospital sites and showed that there are opportunities for us to improve how we use them.

"These proposed changes will help us ensure our patients receive the right care, at the right time, in the right unit; and reduce the time they have to stay in hospital."

He said staff had been consulted, as per trust policy, to give them the opportunity "to shape future plans".

# Trust faces council probe over ward shake-up plans

![A blue and white sign reading Furness General Hospital is on the grass in the foreground. One of the hospital facilities is at the back. It is a two-storey brown brick building. There is another sign near it with directions to different parts of the hospital. The sky is grey. ](https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/ace/standard/562/cpsprodpb/7467/live/3cb6f970-a827-11ef-9c9f-85cfc89e7be6.jpg)Image source, LDRSImage caption, 

A council scrutiny committee will also meet to discuss the review of services at Furness General Hospital

Dan HuntLocal Democracy Reporting ServicePamela BilalovaBBC News, North East and Cumbria

- Published22 November 2024

**A hospital trust's decision to potentially close or repurpose several wards without a public consultation is to be investigated by a group of councillors.**

Furness General Hospital, in Barrow, Cumbria, faces proposed changes as part of a review of beds at the site.

University Hospitals of Morecambe Bay NHS Foundation Trust (UHMBT) said no consultation was needed because there were no changes or reductions to services, although locations may change to improve patient care.

Furness Locality Board - which ensures "local voices are heard" - said it would start an investigation early in December about why public talks did not take place.

Westmorland and Furness councillors have also called an "extraordinary meeting" of the adults and health scrutiny committee over "significant concerns" about the planned shake-up.

## 'Proper scrutiny'

Liberal Democrat Dyan Jones, committee chair, said members found out about the plans shortly before information became public.

"In these circumstances we felt it was necessary to take the unusual step of calling an extraordinary meeting to ensure these plans could be properly scrutinised," she said.

At Wednesday's meeting, NHS representatives will be able to provide more details and explain the reasoning for their approach to public engagement.

The committee will consider whether to make recommendations for action, the [Local Democracy Reporting Service](/lnp/ldrs) said.

Labour's Tony Callister, chair of Furness Locality Board, said he welcomed the meeting ahead of its own investigation.

Aaron Cummins, trust chief executive, said: "This review looked at how our beds are configured across the three main hospital sites and showed that there are opportunities for us to improve how we use them.

"These proposed changes will help us ensure our patients receive the right care, at the right time, in the right unit; and reduce the time they have to stay in hospital."

He said staff had been consulted, as per trust policy, to give them the opportunity "to shape future plans".
